experiences.intentions:
Player 3: I am Merlin and know players 1 and 2 are Evil. The proposed team [0,1] includes a known Evil player, so for Good the optimal vote is to reject. Rejecting reveals limited information; approving would risk an immediate failed quest and give Evil momentum.
Player 4: No concrete role information yet from quest outcomes or voting patterns. As a Good Servant on round 1, approving an initial 2-player team is standard to gather information, especially since 5 consecutive rejections auto-pass and early approvals create data from quest result plus future voting. My vote reveals little individually and helps move the game forward.
Player 2: I know Player 1 is my evil teammate, and this proposed team [0,1] contains exactly one evil (1). As evil in the team voting phase, approving a team with my teammate on it is strong because it gives evil a chance to fail Quest 1 while looking plausible this early. My vote itself reveals little since early votes are noisy, and approving helps my side more than rejecting here.
Player 0: No concrete role information exists yet from round 1, quest 1. As Good servant, early game on a 2-player team usually favors approving reasonable opening teams because they generate information and avoid needless rejection pressure. Team [0,1] includes me, which slightly lowers sabotage risk from my seat, and rejecting gives little value with no prior evidence. Approving reveals a standard pro-information stance and does not significantly hurt Good.
Player 1: I know player 2 is my evil teammate; players 0, 3, and 4 are not confirmed beyond likely good. The phase is team voting on my own proposed team [0,1] for quest 1. As evil Assassin, approving a team with only me as evil gives me direct control to fail if desired, and approving my own reasonable-looking team avoids suspicious inconsistency. This reveals little since leaders often approve their own first-round teams, and a later fail can create uncertainty between me and player 0.

The current phase is team voting, and as Good/Merlin I should reject any team containing known evil players, especially a team containing both 1 and 2. Approving would risk another failed quest and gives Evil momentum; rejecting reveals only that I dislike this suspicious team, which is worth it.
Player 4: Quest 1 team [0,1] failed with exactly 1 fail, so at least one of 0 or 1 is evil. As a good Servant, approving a team that includes both suspects plus an unknown 2 is too risky. Player 1: I know player 2 is evil with me. Quest 1 team [0,1] had exactly 1 fail, which from my perspective is consistent with me failing and does not clear 0. Proposed team [0,1,2] contains both evils, so if approved we can guarantee a fail on quest 2 and go up 2-0. As evil, approving a team with both evils is optimal. My vote reveals little because many players may approve a team including prior members after a fail while trying to test combinations; even if scrutinized, securing the quest matters more.
Player 2: I know Player 1 is my evil teammate, and the first quest team [0,1] failed with exactly one fail, which strongly implicates 1 to observant players. Now the proposed team [0,1,2] contains both evil players. As evil, the optimal play is to approve a team with both evils because it gives us strong control over the quest and a good chance to secure another fail while creating some ambiguity about which of 1 or 2 is evil. Approving is consistent with wanting this team to go through; rejecting would be odd as the proposer and could reduce our chance to capitalize on this favorable team. My vote reveals little beyond support for my own proposal, which is natural.

experiences.intentions:
Player 3: I am Merlin and know 1 and 2 are Evil, so the safest available team is [3,4], which excludes known Evil players. In team voting, I should approve good-leaning teams and reject teams containing known Evil. Approving this reveals only that I support a clean team, which is normal and not too informative compared with rejecting it.
Player 0: Quest 1 [0,1] failed, so at least one of 0/1 is evil; since I know I am good, Player 1 is evil. Quest 2 [0,3,4] succeeded, so 3 and 4 are both likely good. Current phase is team voting on [3,4] for a 2-person mission; approving sends the two strongest good candidates based on results. My vote reveals I trust the clean success team members, which helps good coordination.
Player 4: Quest 1 [0,1] failed, so at least one of 0/1 is evil. Quest 2 [0,3,4] succeeded, which makes 3 and 4 look good and suggests 0 could still be good but doesn't clear them fully. As a good Servant on quest 3, the safest 2-person team is [3,4] because both were on a clean success and neither was on the failed [0,1] team. Approving minimizes risk; rejecting would only delay a strong team and reveal unnecessary doubt about one of the best available pairings.
Player 1: I know player 2 is my evil teammate. Quest 1 with [0,1] failed with exactly one fail, which I likely caused, making me somewhat suspicious if others track it. Quest 2 with [0,3,4] succeeded, so at least one of 3 or 4 is likely good, and since 2 was excluded that team was all good if 0 is also good. Current phase is team voting on [3,4] for a 2-person quest. As evil, I want to stop likely-good teams from going. This team excludes both evil players and is very likely to succeed, giving good a 2-1 lead. Rejecting is optimal. My reject aligns with an evil agenda but can be justified since a 2-person team with neither prior fail participant nor broader coverage is narrow; still, stopping this success helps evil more than the information cost hurts.
Player 2: I know Player 1 is my evil teammate. Quest 1 on [0,1] failed with exactly one fail, so either 1 used fail or 0 is also evil; from my knowledge 0 is not my visible teammate, so 1 likely failed and 0 may be good. Quest 2 on [0,3,4] succeeded with no fail, which makes 3 and 4 look relatively good, though 0 could still be evil choosing to pass. Current phase is team voting on [3,4] for a 2-person quest. As evil, I want a team likely to succeed if it excludes evil, because approving a clean team can build trust and this team contains neither me nor my teammate. Rejecting could look suspicious given [3,4] just succeeded. My vote is private until tally context, and approving helps avoid outing myself while allowing a likely good team to take a success, keeping suspicion diffuse for later rounds.

experiences.intentions:
Player 3: I know 1 and 2 are evil, so the proposed team [0,3,4] contains none of the known evil players and matches the successful Quest 3 team. In team voting on Quest 4, as Merlin I want to approve a clean team that can likely secure a third good success while avoiding giving away too much; rejecting a strong all-good-looking team would be suspicious and risks later bad teams. Approving reveals little beyond supporting a proven safe composition, which helps Good more than it hurts.
Player 2: I know Player 1 is my evil teammate; team [0,3,4] excludes both evil players, and it already produced a Success on quest 2, making it a strong likely-good bloc. On quest 4, evil wants to stop Good from reaching 3 successes, so the optimal play is to reject a team that likely contains no evil. My vote reveals some suspicion of this clean team, but as evil I should oppose likely-good teams and try to force a different composition later.
Player 0: Quest 1 team [0,1,2] had exactly 1 fail, so at least one of 1/2 is evil; I know I am good, so suspicion is on 1 or 2. Teams [0,3,4] and [3,4] both succeeded with 0 fails, strongly suggesting 3 and 4 are good. For Quest 4, approving [0,3,4] is optimal for good because it is composed entirely of the strongest good reads and repeating a proven clean core minimizes risk. Approving reveals consistency with prior evidence and helps the good team more than rejecting.
Player 1: I know player 2 is evil with me, and this proposed team [0,3,4] contains no evil players. That means if approved, quest 4 is very likely to succeed, putting Good to match point at 3 successes. As evil in team voting, I should prefer teams containing at least one evil player and reject all-good teams when possible. My vote reveals limited information since evil can plausibly reject a team that excludes them, and there is already some suspicion distribution from prior votes/results. Rejecting best serves evil by trying to force a different team later that includes 1 or 2.
Player 4: I am Good Servant. Known info: Quest 1 [0,1,2] had 1 fail, so at least one of 0/1/2 is evil. Quest 2 [0,3,4] succeeded, so likely 3 and 4 are good; 0 could still be evil but on a 3-person quest evil may choose to pass. Quest 3 [3,4] succeeded, strongly confirming 3 and 4 are good. Current proposed team [0,3,4] repeats the successful Q2 team and includes the confirmed good core 3,4. For Good on quest 4, approving a likely-safe team is optimal because one more success wins and 0 is the least-bad third option from evidence. My vote reveals trust in the strongest available team and helps Good push a probable win.
